input_messages.content:
Based on the evaluation, I need to propose a second mitigation since the first one failed. The previous mitigation (FortiGate rate limiting) was suggested but not properly implemented. Now I need to suggest a different mitigation approach.

Looking at the attack: It's an ICMP flood from UbuntuComputer (192.168.2.3) to external IP 203.0.113.1. The attacker used `hping3 --icmp -i u100000 -c 100 203.0.113.1`. 

Since the FortiGate approach failed, I need a different technical approach. One option is to use the available network devices (Cisco routers/switches) to implement ICMP rate limiting or filtering. Another is to configure the Cisco switches to implement storm control or rate limiting at the network layer.

The UbuntuComputer is connected to cisco-switch-2 (Gi0/1), which then connects through cisco-router-R2 to the FortiGate. I can implement rate limiting on the switch port connecting to UbuntuComputer.

Looking at previous responses, I only suggested FortiGate configuration, so I need a different approach now.

{
  "suggestions": ["Configure storm control and rate limiting on cisco-switch-2 port Gi0/1 (connected to UbuntuComputer) to limit ICMP traffic", "Configure access control lists (ACLs) on cisco-router-R2 to limit ICMP traffic to external networks", "Implement egress filtering on the network boundary to restrict ICMP traffic to external destinations"],
  "chosen_mitigation": "Configure storm control and rate limiting on cisco-switch-2 port Gi0/1 (connected to UbuntuComputer) to limit ICMP traffic",
  "no_mitigation_left": false
}
